artifacts.summary:
## Overview
The Children, Young People and Education Committee held an evidence session with David Blaney, Chief Executive of the Higher Education Funding Council for Wales (HEFCW), and Bethan Owen, Deputy Chief Executive of HEFCW. The discussion focused on the financial sustainability of the higher education sector in Wales, including the impact of demographic changes, pension costs, and Brexit uncertainty. The committee received an update on HEFCW's funding allocations and priorities for the 2019-20 academic year.

## Key Discussion Points
### Financial Sustainability
* The higher education sector in Wales is facing significant financial challenges, including a demographic dip and increased pension costs.
* Universities are taking out cost by reducing capacity, which may have a medium to longer-term impact on the system's ability to deliver for Wales.
* HEFCW is monitoring the sector's financial situation and has a toolkit to help governing bodies make informed decisions.

### Governance
* The committee discussed the importance of governance in universities, including the role of governing bodies and the need for constructive challenge.
* HEFCW has encouraged the sector to engage in an independent review of governance in Wales, which will be supported by HEFCW.

### Funding Allocations
* HEFCW has allocated £149 million for the 2019-20 academic year, including £7 million for recurrent teaching funding and £26 million for part-time funding.
* The committee discussed the importance of maintaining QR funding in real terms and the need for more transparency in the use of fee income.

### Research and Innovation
* The committee discussed the impact of Brexit on research funding and the need for universities to be better embedded in UK-wide conversations.
* HEFCW has committed to providing £15 million in innovation funding to support universities in accessing UKRI funding.

### Part-time Student Funding
* The committee discussed the growth in part-time student numbers in Wales and the need for sustainable funding arrangements.
* HEFCW has allocated £26.5 million for part-time provision in 2019-20 and intends to continue supporting growth in this area.

## Decisions Made
* HEFCW will continue to support and fund growth in part-time provision.
* The sector will work to improve the accessibility of information on university income and expenditure.
* HEFCW will provide £15 million in innovation funding to support universities in accessing UKRI funding.

## Open Questions
* The committee noted that the sector is facing significant financial challenges and that HEFCW is monitoring the situation closely.
* The committee asked for more information on the impact of demographic changes and pension costs on the sector.
* The committee also asked for more information on the governance arrangements in universities and the role of governing bodies.
artifacts.followups:
## Email
Subject: Follow-up on Higher Education Funding Council for Wales Meeting

Dear All,

This email serves as a follow-up to the meeting held on [date] with the Higher Education Funding Council for Wales (HEFCW). The meeting discussed the financial sustainability of the higher education sector in Wales, including the impact of demographic changes, pension costs, and Brexit uncertainty.

Key decisions and action items from the meeting include:

* HEFCW will continue to support and fund growth in part-time provision.
* The sector will work to improve the accessibility of information on university income and expenditure.
* HEFCW will provide £15 million in innovation funding to support universities in accessing UKRI funding.

We will continue to monitor the sector's financial sustainability and work with HEFCW to address any challenges that may arise.

Best regards,
[Your Name]

## Slack
HEFCW has committed to supporting part-time provision growth and improving transparency around university income and expenditure. They'll also provide £15m in innovation funding to help universities access UKRI funding.
artifacts.brief:
COORDINATION BRIEF

PARTICIPANTS:
- Lynne Neagle AM (Committee Chair)
- Angela Burns AM
- Hefin David AM
- Siân Gwenllian AM
- Janet Finch-Saunders AM
- Dr David Blaney (Chief Executive, Higher Education Funding Council for Wales)
- Bethan Owen (Deputy Chief Executive, Higher Education Funding Council for Wales)

SCOPE:
The meeting discusses the financial sustainability of the higher education sector in Wales, including the impact of demographic changes, pension costs, and Brexit uncertainty. The Higher Education Funding Council for Wales (HEFCW) provides an update on their funding allocations and priorities for the 2019-20 academic year.

DECISION CUES:
- HEFCW will continue to support and fund growth in part-time provision.
- The sector will work to improve the accessibility of information on university income and expenditure.
- HEFCW will provide £15 million in innovation funding to support universities in accessing UKRI funding.

CAUTIONS:
- The sector is facing significant financial challenges, including a demographic dip and increased pension costs.
- There is a risk of universities taking on imprudent levels of debt to finance their estates.
- The impact of Brexit on research funding is uncertain, and HEFCW is working to mitigate this risk.
- The governing body of Trinity Saint David has taken decisions that have led to material uncertainty, but HEFCW does not see this as a governance failure.
